coredata.dc:title: Facile and ultrasensitive fluorescence sensor platform for tumor invasive biomaker β-glucuronidase detection and inhibitor evaluation with carbon quantum dots based on inner-filter effect
coredata.dc:creator: Lu, Shuaimin
Li, Guoliang
Lv, Zhengxian
Qiu, Nannan
Kong, Weiheng
Gong, Peiwei
Chen, Guang
Xia, Lian
Guo, Xiaoxi
You, Jinmao
Wu, Yongning
coredata.dc:description: Early detection and diagnosis have great practical significances for the effective prevention and treatment of cancer. In this study, we developed a novel, facile and ultra-sensitive fluorescence assay for the determination of tumor invasive biomarker β-glucuronidase (GLU) based on the inner-filter effect (IFE). The nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) with green photoluminescence were employed as the fluorophore in IFE, and 4-nitrophenyl-β-D-glucuronide (PNPG) was used to act as GLU substrate, and GLU catalytic product (p-nitrophenol (PNP)) was capable of acting as the robust absorber in IFE to turn off the fluorescence of N-CQDs due to the complementary overlap between the absorption of PNP and the excitation of N-CQDs. Thus, signal of GLU activity could be recorded by the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs. Unlike other fluorescence sensing mechanism such as fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) or photoinduced electron transfer (PET), IFE has no requirement for electron or energy transfer process or any chemical modification of fluorophore, which makes our assay more flexible and simple. The proposed method exhibited a good linear relationship from 1UL−1 to 60UL−1 (R2=0.9967) with a low detection limit of 0.3UL−1. This method was also successfully applied to the analysis of serum samples and the inhibitor screening from natural product. The developed sensor platform was proven to be reliable, facile, sensitive, and selective, making it promising as a candidate for GLU activity detection in clinic tumor diagnose and anti-tumor drug screening.
coredata.dcterms:subject: β-glucuronidase
Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots
Inner-filter effect
Anti-tumor drug screening

GLU activity assay in serum samples
Fluorescence sensing mechanism based on inner-filter effect
Application to clinical human serum samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Project funded by China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (No 2016M590071), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 31301595 , 21475074 , 21475075 and 21275089 ), the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province, China ( ZR2013BQ019 ), Experimental technology research projects of QFNU ( SJ201505 ) and Ph.D. research start-up funds of Qufu Normal University ( bsqd 2012017 ). Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The photo of the enzyme reaction solutions with the addition of varying concentration of GLU (A) and UV−vis absorption spectra of the enzymatic reaction solutions upon various levels of GLU activity (B). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Fluorescence intensities of N-CQDs upon addition of various concentrations of GLU (A). The concentrations of GLU were 0, 1, 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 80, 100, 200, 400, 600UL−1, respectively. Plots of fluorescence quenching efficiency value (I 0−I)/I 0 versus GLU activity (B, Inset: linear curve). I 0 and I were the fluorescence intensities of N-CQDs in the absence and presence of GLU, respectively. Error bar represented the standard deviation (n=3).

Analytical results of the proposed method and the colorimetric method in clinic (1–6 represented healthy serum samples, 7–14 represented cancer serum samples). Error bars indicated standard deviations (n=3). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Schematic illustration of fluorescence sensor platform for tumor invasive biomaker β-glucuronidase detection with N-CQDs based on inner-filter effect.

head.ce:title: Facile and ultrasensitive fluorescence sensor platform for tumor invasive biomaker β-glucuronidase detection and inhibitor evaluation with carbon quantum dots based on inner-filter effect

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Life-Organic Analysis of Shandong Province, Qufu Normal University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Early detection and diagnosis have great practical significances for the effective prevention and treatment of cancer. In this study, we developed a novel, facile and ultra-sensitive fluorescence assay for the determination of tumor invasive biomarker β-glucuronidase (GLU) based on the inner-filter effect (IFE). The nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) with green photoluminescence were employed as the fluorophore in IFE, and 4-nitrophenyl-β-D-glucuronide (PNPG) was used to act as GLU substrate, and GLU catalytic product (p-nitrophenol (PNP)) was capable of acting as the robust absorber in IFE to turn off the fluorescence of N-CQDs due to the complementary overlap between the absorption of PNP and the excitation of N-CQDs. Thus, signal of GLU activity could be recorded by the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs. Unlike other fluorescence sensing mechanism such as fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) or photoinduced electron transfer (PET), IFE has no requirement for electron or energy transfer process or any chemical modification of fluorophore, which makes our assay more flexible and simple. The proposed method exhibited a good linear relationship from 1UL−1 to 60UL−1 (R2=0.9967) with a low detection limit of 0.3UL−1. This method was also successfully applied to the analysis of serum samples and the inhibitor screening from natural product. The developed sensor platform was proven to be reliable, facile, sensitive, and selective, making it promising as a candidate for GLU activity detection in clinic tumor diagnose and anti-tumor drug screening.

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer, as a leading cause of deaths in the world, has seriously threatened human life. Most cancer diagnosis is usually too late that misses the best period for treatment. In recent years, many tumor markers have been found and widely used in cancer diagnosis such as the enzymes related to tumor (Chen et al., 2015; Wu and Qu, 2015). β-glucuronidase (GLU) is a member of the lysosomal glycosidase family that catalyzes degradation of glycosaminoglycans of the cell membranes and extracellular matrix of normal and cancerous tissues, which is necessary to the invasion, metastasis and proliferation of tumor cells (Khan et al., 2011; Sperker et al., 1997). Increased activities of GLU have been earlier reported in several cancers, such as colon carcinoma (Goldin and Gorbach, 1976; Kim and Jin, 2001), liver cancer (Bohnenstengel et al., 1999; Cheng et al., 2012), neoplasm of bladder (Hradec et al., 1965), renal carcinoma (Bohnenstengel et al., 1999) and so on. Hence, GLU has been used as a tumor marker (Juan et al., 2009; Mürdter et al., 2002), and the quantitative detection of GLU has become an effective screening tool for the early-stage diagnosis, real-time monitoring, and evaluating clinical therapies. In addition, many evidences show that numerous physiological diseases including rheumatoid arthritis (Falkenbach et al., 1993), urinary tract infection (Ronald et al., 1971), epilepsy (Plum, 1966), and renal diseases (Gonick et al., 1973) are also associated with significantly elevated serum GLU levels. Therefore, it is of great significance to develop a facile and sensitive fluorescent sensing strategy for GLU detection in cancer diagnosis.

Currently, the detection of GLU has attracted widespread concern, but the sensitive methods for its determination remains poorly investigated. Among the available quantitative detection methods, colorimetric method (Waszkiewicz et al., 2015) and chemosensor with organic fluorescent probes (Cheng et al., 2012) or fluorogenic substrates (Liebherr et al., 2014) are the most commonly used strategies. Nevertheless, the colorimetric method often suffers from more or less inherent shortcomings such as low detection sensitivity, poor repeatability or complicated interference factor. For example, some interfering substance such as autoantibodies and heterophilic antibodies can result in the false evaluation of serum GLU levels (Omar, 2012). To overcome these problems, a series of organic fluorescent probes or fluorogenic substrates have been developed to increase the detection sensitivity and accuracy. However, the synthesis of these fluorescent probes or fluorogenic substrates is often complex and extraordinarily time consuming, and not only that, the poor water solubility has been a headache. In order to solve this problem, dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) is often employed, which is harmful for the enzymatic activity, and can greatly reduce the sensitivity and accuracy of the method. Thus, development of the sensitive and effective approaches towards GLU analysis remains great desirable.

Recently, carbon quantum dots (so-called CQDs) as a novel carbon-based material has attracted explosive attention owning to its acknowledged strengths such as good aqueous solubility, excellent biocompatibility, chemical inertness, easily synthesized, low cost and nontoxicity (Dong et al., 2012; Lim et al., 2015; Qu et al., 2016). These performances enable application of CQDs for the optical sensing of many biologically important analytes (Qu et al., 2016). And the sensing mechanisms are mainly involved fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), photoinduced electron transfer (PET), electronic energy transfer (EET), and intramolecular charge transfer (ICT). It is noted that the designs of the nanosensor or chemosensor based on the above mechanisms often require a particular distance between a quencher (or a receptor) and a fluorophore to realize electron or energy transfer process, which make them being time-consuming, costly and complicated, and seriously limit their practical applications. An alternative way to design fluorescent assays is based on the inner-filter effect (IFE), which would occur when the absorption spectrum of interference absorbers in the detection system overlaps with the fluorescence excitation or emission spectra of fluorophore (Li et al., 2016; Zheng et al., 2013). Thus, the absorption response will be transformed exponentially into fluorescence intensity changes, which makes fluorescence analysis more facile and sensitive. Due to the enhancement of the sensitivity and selectivity, IFE could be competitive with other conventional sensing mechanisms because of no requirements for the modification of nanosensor (Jiang et al., 2016; Yan et al., 2015). To the best of our knowledge, there is no trial of development of CQDs-based nanosensor via IFE for GLU activity sensing.

In this study, a novel N-CQDs-based method was firstly designed for facile, rapid and ultrasensitive assay for GLU activity detection based on the IFE. The designed assay principle was illustrated in Scheme 1. The N-CQDs was obtained by one-pot pyrolysis process in our laboratory with strong green photoluminescence and a high fluorescence yield of about 49.5% (Li et al., 2016). In the present study, N-CQDs as an outstanding fluorescent material were used as fluorophore in the IFE based sensing strategy. 4-nitrophenyl-β-D-glucuronide (PNPG) was chosen as substrate of GLU, and its hydrolysate of p-nitrophenol (PNP) by GLU was adopted as the powerful IFE-absorber to affect the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs. Furthermore, as an important indicator in cancer early-stage diagnosis, the investigation of GLU inhibitor is of great significance to medicine discovery. It is due to that the inhibition of GLU activity can effectively control the proliferation of tumor cells. In recent years, novel inhibitor screening from natural products has attracted rapidly growing attention because of their lower side effects than chemical drugs (Gidaro et al., 2015). Thus, the established method was also successfully employed to the screening of GLU inhibitors from natural products. The proposed sensing method was proven to enjoy the merits of superior sensitivity, high selectivity and considerable simplicity, showing a great promising in clinic diagnostic and drug screening.
All the needed materials and used apparatus were shown in Supporting information .
The detailed information for synthesis and purification were presented in Supporting information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection for GLU activity and inhibitor investigation
ce:section.ce:para: A fluorescent GLU activity assay in NaAc-HAc buffer (0.1M, pH 5.0) was performed by adding a series of concentrations of GLU ranging from 1 to 600UL−1 into 300µL 0.001M PNPG solution. After incubation for 30min at 37°C, 350µL 0.1molL−1 sodium hydroxide solution was introduced to make the final solution to alkaline. Then 100µL of mixture was added into 1mL N-CQDs and the fluorescence spectra were recorded with an excitation wavelength of 410nm. For the selectivity of GLU detection, a series of interferences were investigated following the same procedure and the detailed information was presented in Supporting information. All experiments were performed at room temperature and conducted in triplicate.

For the screening of GLU inhibitor, various inhibitors were mixed with GLU (20UL−1) and PNPG solution in a 37°C water bath. After incubation for 30min, the solution was adjusted to alkaline and mixed with N-CQDs solution. Then the resulting mixtures were fully blending and monitored for fluorescent measurements.

ce:section.ce:section-title: GLU activity assay in serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: Serum samples including eight cancer samples and six healthy samples were collected from the First Hospital of Qufu (Shandong, China) and stored frozen before use. In real sample analysis, 10µL serums were added to replace GLU solution, and other procedures were carried out as the above GLU assay. Meanwhile, the serum samples were also analyzed by common colorimetric method in clinic (Marciniak et al., 2006).

As shown in Fig. S1A, the excellent optical characteristics of N-CQDs were observed by UV–vis absorption and photoluminescence emission spectroscopy. The maximum absorption peak and emission wavelength of the N-CQDs were at 410 and 510nm, respectively. From the inset in Fig. S1A, the N-CQDs exhibited strong green fluorescence under 365nm UV light. Moreover, the morphology and microstructure characterized by TEM was presented in Fig. S1B. As we can see, the as-prepared N-CQDs were well-defined nanoparticles with regular sphericity, and had a narrow size range of 5–7nm. XPS, XRD and FT-IR spectroscopy were also employed to characterize the as-prepared samples and the result was presented in Supporting information. From the above results, we confirm that the synthesized N-CQDs have rich hydrophilic groups including hydroxyl, carbonyl group and amine group, ensuring the good water solubility.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence sensing mechanism based on inner-filter effect
ce:section.ce:para: It is well known that IFE is usually considered as a possible source of error in spectrofluorometry. Interestingly, IFE has been employed as an effective tool for simple and facile detection of target analytes by converting the analytical absorption signals into fluorescence signals (Barati et al., 2015; Rong et al., 2015). However, because of the limited choice of suitable absorber and fluorophore, the application of IFE-based fluorescence assay is usually restricted to a certain extent. In this study, a novel fluorescence sensing assay for GLU screening had been demonstrated through an N-CQDs-based nanosensor based on the IFE. In this assay, PNPG was chosen as substrate of GLU and PNP (the hydrolysate of PNPG by GLU) was adopted as the IFE-absorber. As the substrate of GLU, PNPG is the most commonly used and it is quite cheap and easy to get. On the other hand, the UV−vis absorption peak of PNPG is at 310nm, which has no effect on fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs. However, the absorption peak of hydrolysate PNP has a good overlap with the excitation spectrum of N-CQDs at 410nm that can significantly affect the fluorescence of N-CQDs due to the IFE (as shown in Fig. S3). Thus, PNPG and its enzyme catalytic product PNP were capable of functioning as the GLU substrate and the absorber in IFE, respectively. To demonstrate the feasibility of the our design, different concentrations of GLU were mixed with 0.001M PNPG dissolved in 0.1M NaAc-HAc buffer (pH 5.0) to incubate at 37°C for 30min, and the UV−vis absorption spectrum and photograph were shown in Fig. 1. As seen from Fig. 1B, the UV absorbance of the reaction solution at 310 and 410nm were, respectively, decreased and increased gradually with the increasing concentration of GLU from 0 to 100UL−1. Meanwhile, due to the formation of PNP, the colors of enzymatic reaction solutions obviously changed from colorless to light yellow, and bright yellow (as shown in Fig. 1A). The color change of PNPG driven by GLU has been used for colorimetric detection of GLU, but the colorimetric method usually gives a lower selectivity and sensitivity than that of fluorescence approaches (Buss et al., 1997). By employing the means of the IFE based principle above, the facile, rapid, selective and ultrasensitive screening of GLU activity can be achieved readily.

To get the best sensing response, the analytical conditions containing reaction temperature, pH and time were optimized systematically. As observed from Fig. S4 , temperature of 37°C, pH of 5.0 and time of 30min were selected as the optimal conditions. Under the optimum conditions, the reaction solutions with different concentrations of GLU were added into 1mL of N-CQDs solution diluted with pH 8.0 PBS buffer to investigate sensitivity of the sensing strategy. As indicated in Fig. 2A, the fluorescence intensity decreased dramatically with the increasing concentration of GLU. And the fluorescence quenching efficiency value ((I 0−I)/I 0) displayed a good linear relationship to the concentration of GLU in the range from 1UL−1 to 60UL−1 and the fitted linear data could be described as Y=0.0086+0.0050 X (UL−1), R 2=0.9967 (Fig. 2B), where I 0 and I are the fluorescence intensities of N-CQDs in the absence and presence of GLU, respectively. The limit of detection (LOD) was estimated to be 0.3UL−1 based on the generalized 3σ method. This sensitivity is enough for GLU activity detection in biological samples (the normal range of serum GLU in adults is about 8.0UL−1). Furthermore, the performance of our method was compared with other methods (Table S1) and the detailed comparison was described in Supporting information .

The selectivity of the proposed sensing approach was further studied using ordinary interfering substances including a number of enzymes, nonspecific proteins, some metal ions and twenty common amino acids. The specific results were described in Supporting information . It was observed from Fig. S5 that under the same conditions, only the targeted GLU presented drastic fluorescence quenching effect, while the other substances showed negligible effect, indicating the satisfactory selectivity of the new sensing method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application to clinical human serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: To further demonstrate the analytical reliability and application potential, the proposed sensing method was employed to detect GLU in clinical human serum samples. Prior to the sample analysis, serum sample was added to the sensing system to explore the matrix effect on fluorescence response. As seen in Fig. S6, there was no evident difference after the addition of serum sample. The serum samples collected from eight cancer patient serums and six healthy serums were screened by using the developed assay protocol, and the results were presented in Fig. 3. On the basis of the calibration curve, the activity of GLU in the serum of cancer patients (17.0±2.2UL−1) was significantly higher in comparison to the healthy individuals (7.4±1.1UL−1), and these results also indicated the GLU activity was close related with tumor and could be competent as a meaningful tumor biomarker. The recovery of added known amounts of GLU to serum samples was ranged from 95.42% to 103.67% (Table S2), indicating the good accuracy of the proposed method. Furthermore, in order to verify the accuracy of the developed method, these samples were also screened by the common colorimetric method in clinic (Marciniak et al., 2006) (Fig. 3). In addition, the correlation analysis between the two methods was explored by Statistical Product and Service Solutions (SPSS). As presented in Fig. S7, a high correlation coefficient (0.9930) was obtained and the correlation was significant with a P value less than 0.01, indicating a satisfactory consistency between the two methods. These experimental results confirmed that the IFE based method was facile and highly sensitive for GLU detection. Furthermore, it is to be noted that the new sensing method provided the satisfactory accuracy with the consumption of 10µL serum sample, and this low sample consumption was of vital importance in clinic tumor studies.

The medicines targeted at GLU played an important role in anti-tumor and other physiological diseases therapy. Herein, the efficient, facile and sensitive GLU assay was utilized to GLU inhibitor investigation (Supporting information). The inhibiting ability of several active compounds including oleanolic acid, ursolic acid, berberine, corynoline, dehydrocorydaline and pomegranate peel extracts obtained with different extraction solvents were screened and their chemical formulas were shown in Fig. S8. The IC50 for the screened compounds was given in Fig. S9. It can be concluded from the result that all these natural bioactive products offered certain GLU inhibiting ability, some results were consistent with the previously reported studies (Cragg et al., 2009). In these compounds, ursolic acid showed the best inhibiting ability, followed by oleanolic acid, berberine, dehydrocorydaline and corynoline. Furthermore, the inhibiting ability of pomegranate peel extracts obtained with different solvents was also screened by the proposed method, and the detailed results were shown in Fig. S9B. Based on the above results, we can safely conclude that our method provides a potential option for medicine discovery from natural product.

We first demonstrated a facile and sensitive method based the IFE for GLU activity sensing and inhibitor screening, which provided a promising platform for GLU sensing in clinical tumor diagnosis and GLU inhibitor screening in drug discovery. Moreover, the proposed IFE based sensing strategy would provide new insights for the development of facile and sensitive sensors for tumor biomarker detection using fluorescence nanomaterials, because of the avoiding the complex modification of the fluorophore or covalent bond linking between a receptor and a fluorophore.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Project funded by China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (No 2016M590071), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 31301595, 21475074, 21475075 and 21275089), the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province, China (ZR2013BQ019), Experimental technology research projects of QFNU (SJ201505) and Ph.D. research start-up funds of Qufu Normal University (bsqd 2012017).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j. bios.2016.05.021.
